coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-induced in situ generation of polymer carbon dots for fluorescence immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guoyong
Zhao, Jiahui
Wang, Shuang
Lu, Shasha
Sun, Jian
Yang, Xiurong
coredata.dc:description: Current fluorescence immunoassays generally employ synthetic fluorophore-labeled antibody and complicated fluorescence regulation process. We are dedicated to developing a conceptual fluorescent enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) based on enzyme-induced in situ fluorogenic reaction to resolve such issues. For the construction of novel fluorescent ELISA, discovering a moderate but rapid fluorogenic reaction and choosing a preferable substrate are both necessary and significant. In this study, we have demonstrated that 4-aminophenol (AP) can react with ethylenediamine at 37 °C for 2 h to produce strong green fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) for the first time. Inspired by this simple reaction and alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-triggered specific hydrolysis of 4-aminophenyl phosphate (APP) into AP, we have developed a strategy for detecting ALP activity via in situ formation of fluorescent PCDs. Furthermore, employing conventional ELISA platform and commercial antibodies to cardiac troponin I (cTnI), we construct a simple and convenient ALP-based fluorescent ELISA for quantitatively evaluating the cTnI level in human serum. Such original ELISA via in situ generation of fluorescent nanomaterials from scratch has great potential for the early diagnosis of disease markers in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Fluorescent ELISA
Polymer carbon dots
Cardiac troponin I

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ALP activity sensing and its inhibitor investigation
Synthesis and optical properties of fluorescent PCDs
Fluorescent sensor for ALP and its inhibitor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences
National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of EA (a), AP (b), fluorescent PCDs (c), and the excitation (d) and emission (e) spectra of the fluorescent PCDs. Inset photos display the fluorescent PCDs under ambient (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), respectively. (B) TEM image of the fluorescent PCDs. Inset shows the size distribution histogram. (C) FT-IR spectra of AP (black line) and the fluorescent PCDs (red line). (D) XPS spectrum of fluorescent PCDs (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) fluorescence intensities at 507 nm of the fluorescent PCDs by adding various concentrations of AP (0–200 μM) into 60 mM EA.

(A) Schematic representation of ALP-induced formation of fluorescent PCDs. (B) Absorption and (C) fluorescence emission spectra of APP (a), APP + EA (b), ALP + EA (c), ALP + APP (d), ALP + APP + EA (e), and as-prepared PCDs (f). The concentrations of APP, EA, and ALP are 1 mM, 60 mM, and 40 mU/mL, respectively. The fluorescent PCDs are obtained by reaction of 300 μM of AP and 60 mM of EA at 37 °C for 2 h.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) fluorescence intensities at 507 nm of the fluorescent PCDs in situ synthesized by various concentrations of ALP. (C) Fluorescence intensities at 507 nm against the logarithm of ALP concentrations. (D) Selectivity investigation of the sensing system for ALP activity (40 mU/mL) assay. The concentrations of non-specific enzymes/proteins, K+, Ca2+, Na+, and other metal ions were 10 μg/mL, 500 μM, and 50 μM, respectively. Inset in part B depicts a linearity between fluorescence intensities at 507 nm and ALP concentrations.

(A) Schematic illustration of cTnI detection via ALP-triggered fluorescent ELISA. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra and (C) corresponding fluorescence intensities at 507 nm of the fluorescent ELISA with various cTnI concentrations. (D) Fluorescence intensities at 507 nm against the logarithm of cTnI concentrations. Inset in part C shows the linearity of the sensing system to cTnI concentrations.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-induced in situ generation of polymer carbon dots for fluorescence immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and convenient fluorescent ELISA based on in situ formation of polymer carbon dots has been constructed for quantitatively evaluating the cTnI level.

Current fluorescence immunoassays generally employ synthetic fluorophore-labeled antibody and complicated fluorescence regulation process. We are dedicated to developing a conceptual fluorescent enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) based on enzyme-induced in situ fluorogenic reaction to resolve such issues. For the construction of novel fluorescent ELISA, discovering a moderate but rapid fluorogenic reaction and choosing a preferable substrate are both necessary and significant. In this study, we have demonstrated that 4-aminophenol (AP) can react with ethylenediamine at 37 °C for 2 h to produce strong green fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) for the first time. Inspired by this simple reaction and alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-triggered specific hydrolysis of 4-aminophenyl phosphate (APP) into AP, we have developed a strategy for detecting ALP activity via in situ formation of fluorescent PCDs. Furthermore, employing conventional ELISA platform and commercial antibodies to cardiac troponin I (cTnI), we construct a simple and convenient ALP-based fluorescent ELISA for quantitatively evaluating the cTnI level in human serum. Such original ELISA via in situ generation of fluorescent nanomaterials from scratch has great potential for the early diagnosis of disease markers in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: In 1971, Engvall and Weeman developed enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) that relied on catalytic amplification of enzyme and specific recognition between antigen and antibody [1,2]. Now, because of its high throughput, specificity, and low cost, ELISA has been extensively utilized in clinical diagnosis [3], environmental protection [4], food safety [5], and laboratory study [6]. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) has been widely used in ELISA as a labeling enzyme due to its good stability, easy binding to antibodies, broad substrate, and high turnover number [7–9]. In addition, ALP can hydrolyze phosphate group in alkaline media and play a vital role in phosphate metabolism [10,11]. The aberrant expression of ALP may cause hepatitis [12], prostatic cancer [13], and bone disease [14]. Given the significance of ALP in pathophysiology and ELISA, the determination of its activity is important not only for ALP-associated diseases but also for ALP-based ELISA platforms.

Up to now, several methods, including fluorometry, electrochemistry, colorimetry, surface enhancement Raman scattering, and chemiluminescence, have been applied to monitor ALP activity [15]. As for ELISA, the conventional colorimetric ELISA suffers from poor sensitivity because of the limitation of amplified signal. By changing the output signal, a variety of ELISAs have been established based on conventional ELISA to solve this problem [16]. For example, plasmonic ELISA based on noble metals etching [17], deposition [18], or aggregation [19] developed rapidly to improve the sensitivity of detection. However, it takes a lot of time for the synthesis or modification of these noble nanomaterials. Moreover, noble nanomaterials are vulnerable to external factors [20–22]. As an alternative to colorimetric ELISA, fluorescent ELISA has gradually attracted attention because of its inherent selectivity/high sensitivity, fast response, and simple operation [23]. However, current ALP-based fluorescent ELISAs are quite limited, because most of them rely on the substrates ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) [24–27], and p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) [28,29]. To promote the development of fluorescent ELISA, it is necessary to introduce new substrate and fluorogenic reaction to establish a simple and convenient ALP-based fluorescent ELISA.

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), including polymer carbon dots (PCDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have drawn wide attention owning to their fascinating properties, such as high chemical stability, good water solubility, excellent optical, low toxicity, and superior biocompatibility [30]. The current synthesis of CDs mostly relies on hydrothermal or solvothermal at high temperature and high pressure [31]. Ethylenediamine (EA) is a widely used raw material for synthetic CDs [32]. However, there are a few reports on the synthesis of CDs using EA as raw material at moderate or room temperature. In this regard, Han et al. prepared multicolor photoluminescent PCDs using hydroquinone (HQ) and EA at 50 °C [33]. Zhang et al. spent 16 h to prepare green-emissive CDs using p-benzoquinone (BQ) and EA at room temperature [34]. Inspired by such synthetic method, we have used 4-aminophenol (AP) instead of BQ or HQ in the synthesis of CDs and found that AP could react with EA at 37 °C for 2 h to produce strong green fluorescent PCDs. This simple, cheap and mild reaction encourages us to apply it to fluorescence immunoassay, although the application of CDs in fluorescence immunoassay is still in its infancy [35].

Here, we propose a simple and convenient ALP-induced fluorescent ELISA via in situ formation of PCDs. In this strategy, the substrate of ALP is 4-aminophenyl phosphate (APP), because it could not react with EA to produce fluorescence. However, ALP can remove phosphate group from APP to produce AP, which reacts with EA to give green fluorescent PCDs, and the fluorescence intensity is related to the ALP activity. The ALP-labeled secondary antibody recognizes the primary antibody, and the primary antibody recognizes the target antigen that binds to the capture antibody on the well plate. Therefore, the target antigen concentration establishes an indirect relationship with the fluorescence intensity [36]. As a proof of concept, cardiac troponin I (cTnI), a well-known biomarker of acute myocardial infarction (AMI), is selected as the target antigen to study the robustness and efficiency in this fluorescent ELISA. Our immunosensor exhibits the advantages of easy operation, low cost, and simple signal generation, which has great potential in disease diagnosis.

4-aminophenol (AP) and ethylenediamine (EA) were bought from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). 4-Aminophenyl phosphate monosodium salt hydrate (APP), sodium orthovanadate (Na3VO4), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) from bovine intestinal mucosa, glucose oxidase (GOx), bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), trypsin, lysozyme, and EcoR I were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (Shanghai, China). Human immunoglobulin G (IgG) and TBST were obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Corporation (Shanghai, China). Cardiac troponin I (cTnI), cardiac troponin T (cTnT), carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA), human alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), mouse anti-cTnI antibody, rabbit anti-cTnI antibody (Ab1), and ALP-conjugated goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody (ALP-Ab2) were supplied by Abcam Corporation (Cambridge, UK). Human serum samples were supplied by the China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, China). Other inorganic salts were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Corporation (China). All chemicals were analytical grade and were used as received. Milli-Q purified water (18.2 MΩ cm) is used in all aqueous solutions.

The Hitachi F-4600 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan), CARY 500 UV–vis-NIR Varian spectrophotometer (CA, USA), and Bruker Optics VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Ettlingen, Germany) were utilized to record fluorescence spectra, UV–vis spectra, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the fluorescent PCDs was obtained on a Tecnai G2 F20 instrument (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements and MALDI-TOF mass spectrum were measured on Thermo ESCALAB VG Scientific 250 (West Sussex, UK) and autoflex III smart beam MALDI-TOF/TOF mass spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), respectively.

200 mg AP was dissolved in 40 mL ultrapure water, followed by adding 10 mL EA. The mixture was allowed to stand at 37 °C for 2 h, during which the solution gradually turned dark brown. The crude solid-state product was obtained by freeze-drying and then washed with acetone for three times. The precipitate was dried at room temperature to obtain the pure solid-state product, which was used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP activity sensing and its inhibitor investigation
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor ALP activity, the following steps were carried out. 45 μL of ALP with different activities, 225 μL of water, and 450 μL of 2 mM APP were added into 180 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM, pH 10) containing 0.1 mM MgCl2. Afterward, the resultant solution was maintained at 37 °C for 60 min, followed by adding 100 μL of 0.6 M EA. The fluorescence spectra measurements were performed after incubation at 37 °C for another 120 min. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at 507 nm with the excitation at 400 nm. Non-specific proteins/enzymes and common metal ions were used to assess the selectivity of the sensing system with/without ALP. To evaluate the sensing system in real samples, we prepared spiked samples by adding various concentrations of ALP to diluted human serum (1%) and then detected ALP activity.

For ALP inhibition assay, 90 μL of Na3VO4 solution with various concentrations ranging for 0–1.5 mM, 450 μL of 2 mM APP, 135 μL of water, and 45 μL of ALP (0.8 U/mL) were introduced into 180 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM, pH 10) containing 0.1 mM MgCl2. The mixed solution was kept at 37 °C for 60 min, followed by adding 100 μL of 0.6 M EA. The fluorescence spectra measurements were performed with the excitation at 400 nm after incubation at 37 °C for another 120 min.

100 μL of 5 μg/mL mouse monoclonal anti-cTnI antibody (capture antibody) was injected into each well of the 96-well plates and then kept at 4 °C overnight. After discarding the solution, the plates were washed thrice with TBST. To prevent nonspecific adsorption, each well was blocked with 3% BSA at 37 °C for 1 h, followed by washing. Following that, 100 μL of cTnI standard solutions with various concentrations ranging from 0 to 250 ng/mL were introduced to the wells immobilized with capture antibody, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 1 h. After washing as before, 100 μL of 2 μg/mL rabbit anti-cTnI antibody was injected into the wells and kept at 37 °C for 1 h. After washing, 100 μL of ALP-conjugated goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody (1:5000) was added into the wells, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 1 h and washing for three times. Then, 112.5 μL of 2 mM APP, 67.5 μL of water, and 45 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM, pH 10) containing 0.1 mM MgCl2 were introduced into each well. After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, 25 μL of 0.6 M EA were injected to the wells. The resultant solution was left for 2 h at 37 °C before fluorescent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of fluorescent PCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed solution of BQ (HQ) and EA can easily produce fluorescent CDs at moderate or room temperature [33,34]. We speculate that the fluorogenic reaction may also occur at moderate or room temperature using analogues of BQ (HQ). Therefore, we choose AP as the carbon source and EA as the reactant to prepare the fluorescent PCDs. As expected, AP can react with EA at 37 °C for 2 h to produce strong green fluorescent PCDs. In addition, EA is a strongly alkaline primary amine and contains two amino groups. However, AP cannot react with other common alkaline solutions (e.g. NaOH, Tris, NH3·H2O, and Na2HPO4) to generate fluorescent signals (Fig. S1A). This results indicate that two amino groups play an important role in the synthesis of fluorescent PCDs.

The optical properties of the fluorescent PCDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, EA has no absorption peak, while AP has two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 300 nm, respectively. Apart from these two absorption peaks, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the fluorescent PCDs appears two new absorption peaks at 335 nm and 400 nm, which can be ascribed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [37]. As shown in inset of Fig. 1A, the fluorescent PCDs solutions present yellow under sunlight and green under 365 nm UV light. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of fluorescent PCDs are at 400 nm and 507 nm (Fig. S1B), respectively. The relative fluorescence quantum yield is 8.54%, as depicted in Fig. S1C.

For a better understanding of the fluorescent PCDs, the TEM, FT-IR, XPS, and MALDI-TOF mass measurements were carried out. The TEM image was utilized to reveal the morphology and size of fluorescent PCDs. The fluorescent PCDs present well-dispersed nanoparticles with distribution in 5–28 nm (Fig. 1B and its insert). As seen from Fig. S1D, the lattice spacing of PCDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [38]. The functional groups on the fluorescent PCDs surface were characterized by FT-IR measurements, as depicted in Fig. 1C. Compared with that of AP, the FT-IR spectrum of the fluorescent PCDs shows a new peak at 1654 cm−1 (CO/CN stretching vibration) [39], and loses one peak at around 888 cm−1 (CH bending vibration of aromatic rings) [40]. These results indicate that AP may react with EA by Schiff base reaction and addition reaction. In addition, the FT-IR measurements by stretching vibration mode demonstrate that the fluorescent PCDs possess other functional groups, such as OH/NH (3280 cm−1), CC (1510 cm−1), CN (1340 cm−1), and CO (1228 cm−1) [41].

XPS measurements were used to characterize the chemical composition of the fluorescent PCDs. As seen from Fig. 1D, three non-hydrogen elements: C (285 eV), N (401 eV), and O (532 eV) can be observed in the full survey of XPS results of the fluorescent PCDs [42]. Fig. S2 shows the high-resolution spectra of C, N, and O. The C 1s spectrum of fluorescent PCDs indicates the existence of CC/CC (284.6 eV), CN/CO (286.0 eV), and CN/CO (288.5 eV) bonds [43]. The N 1s spectrum demonstrates that the fluorescent PCDs possess C=N (398.5 eV), NH (399.0 eV), and CN (400.3 eV) bonds [44]. The O 1s spectrum shows two peaks, representing CO (531.6 eV) and CO (533.0 eV) bonds [45]. The presence of CN bond demonstrates the existence of Schiff base reaction, which is consistent with the FT-IR result.

MALDI-TOF mass spectrum analysis was carried out to investigate the reaction mechanism. As presented in Fig. S3A and B, the four main peaks at m/z 256, 383, 450, and 561 may be ascribed to the possible chemical structures of (1), (2), (3), and (4), respectively. As shown in the chemical structures of (3) and (4) in Fig. S3B, the intramolecular hydrogen bonds through the interaction of NH3 + (derived from EA) and phenyl-O− (derived from AP) may exist in the nanoparticles. The chemical structures of (3) and (4) in Fig. S3B confirm the existence of Schiff base reaction (CN) and Michael addition reaction [CN (produced by the reaction between AP and EA)] [46]. CN (produced by AP itself) demonstrates self-polymerization, as evidenced by the chemical structures of (1), (2), (3), and (4) in Fig. S3B. Therefore, combined with the results of TEM, FT-IR, XPS, MALDI-TOF mass, control experiments, and previous studies, we speculate that the possible reaction mechanism of AP with EA is as follow. First of all, AP is oxidized to quinoneimine by alkaline condition [47]. Next, the quinoneimine will undergo Schiff base reaction and Michael addition reaction with EA, accompanied by a self-polymerization reaction. Finally, the intermediate products [such like (1), (2), (3) and (4)] are polymerized together through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding to form fluorescent PCDs [48].

We studied the effect of EA concentration and reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent PCDs. In Fig. S4, as the EA concentration and the reaction time increase, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then tends to be gentle. To save reagents and time, we chose 60 mM EA and 2 h as reaction parameters. Subsequently, we investigated in detail the relationship between AP and fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the intensities of fluorescence spectra at 507 nm exhibit concentration-dependent increase with increasing AP concentration. There is a good linearity (R2 = 0.990) between AP (0–200 μM) and fluorescence intensity at 507 nm (Fig. 2B), which encourages us to establish a method for detection of AP-related targets.

What is worth mentioning is that AP can be obtained via the hydrolysis of APP by ALP. Moreover, APP cannot react with EA to produce fluorescence signal. Since EA can distinguish between AP and APP, APP is chosen as the substrate for detection of ALP. Next, the feasibility of the experiment would be studied in detail by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 B and C). The solo solution of APP (curve a), the mixed solution of APP and EA (curve b), and the mixed solution of ALP and EA (curve c) do not show absorption at 400 nm and fluorescence at 507 nm, indicating that APP displays no fluorescent signal and EA cannot react with APP or ALP. ALP-treated APP solution exhibits a broad absorption peak from 300 to 700 nm, but no fluorescence peak at 507 nm (curve d). However, when EA is added to ALP-treated solution, two absorption peaks appear at 335 nm and 400 nm, accompanied by a fluorescence peak at 507 nm (curve e). This results are coincide with that of the as-prepared fluorescent PCDs (curve f), indicating that the fluorescent PCDs have been formed under the action of ALP. So far, we have shown that ALP can hydrolyze APP to produce AP, which then reacts with EA to generate fluorescent PCDs (Fig. 3A). Therefore, we propose a new strategy for detecting ALP activity via enzyme-induced in situ production of fluorescent PCDs.

To achieve favorable sensing performance, the sensing conditions were optimized, as depicted in Fig. S5. The optimum reaction conditions of pH, APP concentration, the incubation time of APP with ALP, and the reaction time of ALP-treated APP solution with EA were 10.0, 1 mM, 60 min, and 120 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensor for ALP and its inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, we monitored ALP activity. The fluorescence intensities at 507 nm show concentration-dependent increase with increasing ALP activity (Fig. 4 A). Obviously, ALP activity in the range of 0.1–70 mU/mL and the fluorescence intensities at 507 nm show two good linear relationships, as depicted in Fig. 4B insert and C. One regression equation can be described as I1 = 4.91 [ALP] + 1.31, (R2 = 0.998), with ALP activity in the range of 0.1–6 mU/mL. The other is I2 = −233.95 + 341.30 log [ALP], (R2 = 0.995), with ALP activity in the range of 6–70 mU/mL, where I1 and I2 are the fluorescence intensities at 507 nm. Compared with previously reported assays for ALP (Table S1), a comparable detection limit of 0.1 mU/mL was obtained. To verify the specificity of the sensing system, we chose non-specific proteins/enzymes and common metal ions as interfering substances, which include BSA, GOx, EcoR I, HSA, lysozyme, trypsin, Na+, K+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, and Ag+. The interfering substances with/without ALP cannot cause significant interference to our sensing system (Fig. 4D), indicating that our approach has good selectivity for ALP.

To illustrate the ability of our sensing system in complex biological samples, we measured ALP activity in human serum (1%). The recoveries were between 100.2% and 101.8% with the relative standard deviations less than 5%, indicating that the proposed approach could be applied to monitor ALP activity in real samples (Table S2). In addition, we applied our sensing system to the screening of ALP inhibitors. The well-recognized ALP inhibitor (Na3VO4) was chosen as a model to assess our proposed sensing system for screening ALP inhibitors. As shown in the Fig. S6, with the concentrations of Na3VO4 varying from 1 to 1500 μM, the fluorescence intensities of the test solution decrease gradually. Using a logarithmic concentration of Na3VO4 to plot the fluorescence intensity, we obtained a typical S-curve. The IC50 value (concentration of inhibitor when ALP activity is inhibited by 50%) is approximately 50.0 μM, which has the same order of magnitude as previously reported [49]. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed sensing system for screening ALP inhibitors.

Enlightened by the good performance of ALP detection and the wide application of ALP in various ELISAs, we attempted to construct a fluorescent ELISA via ALP-induced in situ production of PCDs for monitoring target antigen. We chose cTnI, a biomarker of AMI, as the target antigen. When AMI breaks out, the cTnI concentration increases to 50 ng/mL in a short time (3–5 h). The cTnI concentration will increase to 550 ng/mL or higher in the next few days. After 6–8 days, the cTnI concentration returns to normal level (0.5–2 ng/mL). Therefore, accurate determination of the cTnI concentration is of great significance for the early diagnosis of AMI [50]. The ability of the proposed fluorescent ELISA to detect cTnI was investigated with the help of commercial cTnI antibodies. Fig. 5 A is the schematic illustration of the detection of cTnI by the proposed fluorescent ELISA. The procedure is basically the same as that of conventional ELISA. The higher concentration of the cTnI in the solution, the more ALP-Ab2 is. ALP can hydrolyze the substrate APP to produce AP, which then reacts with EA to form fluorescent PCDs. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity is indirectly related to the cTnI concentration.

As seen from Fig. 5B and C, the fluorescence intensities at 507 nm show concentration-dependent increase with increasing cTnI concentration. Two linear relationships between fluorescence intensity at 507 nm and cTnI concentration ranging from 1 to 250 ng/mL can be obtained, as depicted in Fig. 5C insert and D. The regression equations can be described as F1 = 4.60 + 0.81 [cTnI], (R2 = 0.997), with cTnI concentrations in the range of 1–30 ng/mL, and F2 = − 268.72 + 203.0 log [cTnI], (R2 = 0.992), with cTnI concentrations in the range of 30–250 ng/mL, respectively, where F1 and F2 are the fluorescence intensities at 507 nm. Compared with previously reported immunoassays for cTnI (Table S3), a comparable detection limit of 1 ng/mL was acquired. To verify the specificity of the proposed immunosensor, the competitors, including cTnT, CEA, IgG, AFP, HSA, and trypsin, were investigated by our proposed fluorescent ELISA. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the competitors cause no obvious impact on our proposed fluorescent ELISA, indicating that our proposed fluorescent ELISA has good selectivity toward cTnI. Accordingly, with the help of conventional ELISA platform, we have established a simple, inexpensive, efficient, and sensitive fluorescent ELISA via in situ formation of PCDs.

With the encouragement of the good linearity and selectivity of our proposed immunosensor, we utilized our proposed fluorescent ELISA to investigate mimetic samples containing different concentration of cTnI in diluted human serum. At the same time, we also utilized commercial pNPP-based standard ELISA kit to monitor cTnI in mimetic samples. As seen from Table S4, these two methods have no obvious difference in the results measurements. Our proposed fluorescent ELISA can be used as a substitute for conventional colorimetric ELISA in diagnosis of disease biomarkers due to its cheapness, easy operation, and simple signal generation.

In summary, we found a fluorogenic reaction between AP and EA to produce strong green fluorescent PCDs at 37 °C. We have speculated the formation mechanism of green fluorescence PCDs through a series of characterizations (MALDI-TOF mass, XPS, and FT-IR). The main reaction of AP and EA is Schiff base reaction, Michael addition reaction, and self-polymerization. On the other hand, since ALP can catalyze specific conversion of APP into AP and EA can distinguish between AP and APP, we developed a biosensor to detect ALP activity and screen its inhibitor by in situ generation of fluorescent PCDs. With the help of conventional ELISA platform, we have established a novel ALP-triggered fluorescent ELISA via in situ formation of PCDs. Using cTnI as a model target antigen, the fluorescent ELISA exhibited excellent sensing performance for cTnI detection in human serum. Therefore, we envision that this simple, low-cost, and highly efficient fluorescent ELISA may be a valuable tool to monitor clinical disease biomarkers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial supports by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFA0201301), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21435005, 21627808, 21974132), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association, CAS (No. 2018258).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127583.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) quantitative assay of immunoglobulin G

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Engvall, P. Perlmann, Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) quantitative assay of immunoglobulin G, Immunochemistry, 8 (1971), pp. 871-874, 10.1016/0019-2791(71)90454-X
B.K. Van Weemen, A.H.W.M. Schuurs, Immunoassay using antigen-enzyme conjugates, FEBS Lett., 15 (1971), pp. 232-236, 10.1016/0014-5793(71)80319-8

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent development of sandwich assay based on the nanobiotechnologies for proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, and ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immunochemical techniques for multianalyte analysis of chemical residues in food and the environment: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Han, S. Li, Z. Peng, A.M. Othman, R. Leblanc, Recent development of cardiac troponin I detection, ACS Sens., 1 (2016), pp. 106-114, 10.1021/acssensors.5b00318

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guoyong Liu received his bachelor’s degree from Heilongjiang University in 2015. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus mainly on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their application in bioanalysis.

Jiahui Zhao received her bachelor’s and master’s degree from Nanjing University and Jilin University in 2013 and 2016, respectively. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus mainly on the synthesis and design of fluorescent probes and their applications in biomarker detection.

Shuang Wang obtained her bachelor’s degree from Shandong Normal University in 2014. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research is the development of fluorescence sensors and dual polarization interferometry sensing platform for biomarkers detection.

Shasha Lu obtained her bachelor’s degree from Shandong Normal University in 2014. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research is the development of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for biomarkers detection.

Jian Sun is an associate professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research is mainly focused on the biomolecule interaction and the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and their chemosensors application.

Xiurong Yang is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research mainly focuses on design and synthesis of new functional materials with various compositions and morphologies via chemical and physical processes for biomolecules interaction, biosensing and energy storage applications.
